svg.text: MODERN HEBREW
PHONOLOGY — MERGERS AND SIMPLIFICATION
WHAT MODERN HEBREW LOST
Pharyngeal /ʿ/ (ayin): merged with glottal stop / silence for most speakers
Begadkefat fricatives: mostly lost (bet=b=v • kaf=k=kh • pe=p=f remain)
Gimel fricative /ɣ/: gone • Dalet fricative /ð/: gone • Tav /θ/: gone
Long vs short vowels: lost distinction (all vowels now same length)
MODERN HEBREW CONSONANTS (22)
b v g d h w z ḥ t y k x l m n s ʿ p f ts ḳ r sh t
Resh /r/: now uvular trill /ʁ/ (from Yiddish/German influence, not Semitic)
Biblical resh: probably alveolar trill /r/ — reversed by Ashkenazi revival
MODERN HEBREW VOWELS
5 vowels: a e i o u (all short; no length distinction)
Stress: usually penultimate in formal speech; final in colloquial
Schwa /ə/: often deleted in spoken Hebrew (batim not baatim)
ARABIC PHONOLOGY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
MODERN ARABIC DIALECTS: PHONOLOGICAL VARIATION
Egyptian: /q/ → /ʾ/ (glottal) • Levantine: /q/ → /ʾ/ or /g/
Gulf: /q/ preserved as /q/ • Maghrebi: /q/ → /g/ in some dialects
MSA: always /q/ • enormous dialect variation in actual speech
PARALLEL PHONOLOGICAL HISTORY
Modern Hebrew: ayin lost • Egyptian Arabic: /q/ → glottal stop
Both: simplifying their most difficult Semitic consonants in modern use
Modern Hebrew /r/ = uvular • most Arabic /r/ = alveolar (opposite paths!)
HEBREW-ARABIC PHONOLOGICAL CONTACT
Mizrahi Jews (from Arab countries): Hebrew with Arabic phonology
Keep pharyngeal ayin, emphatic consonants, authentic Semitic sounds
Ashkenazi-origin speakers: European phonology imported into Hebrew
Modern Israeli Hebrew: blend of both, still evolving phonologically
PLATE 12b ALT · MODERN HEBREW / ARABIC · PHONOLOGY
